coredata.dc:title: Chitosan and κ-carrageenan-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiyao
Wang, Yesheng
Sun, Lili
Qi, Quan
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: This study develops a high sensitive and selective “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of iron ion (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs), which were synthesized by using chitosan and κ-carrageenan as raw materials through one-step hydrothermal protocol. The synthesized N,S-CDs possess particularly high quantum yield (QY = 59.31%), excellent stability and excitation dependent behavior, showing great potential for practical applications. Furthermore, N,S-CDs provided high selectivity and strong anti-interference to Fe3+ due to its fluorescence quenching performance, revealing a wide linear concentration range from 1 to 100 μM for the detection of Fe3+ ion with an extremely low limit of detection of 57 nM, and presented reliable and accurate results in actual sample detection of Fe3+. The overall fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs with Fe3+ was due to the formation of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ initiated to the aggregation and electron transfer of N,S-CDs, resulting in the static quenching of fluorescence. More interestingly, AA could reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ and efficaciously recover the quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs/Fe3+. N,S-CDs/Fe3+ as “turn-on” fluorescent probe was further applied for detecting AA in a linear range of 0.5–90 μM with a detection limit of 38 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-CDs
κ-Carrageenan
Chitosan
Fe3+ ion detection
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
Possible quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by Fe3+
Fluorescence recovery of N,S-CDs by AA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles ( Qingdao University ) (No. ZKT41 , No. ZFZ201815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HR-TEM images of N,S-CDs. (C) Particle diameter distribution histogram of N,S-CDs. (D) XRD pattern of N,S-CDs. (E) FT-IR spectroscopy of N,S-CDs.
(A) 2D AFM images of N,S-CDs. (B) Corresponding 3D image. (C) Roughness Profile of white line marked in (A).
(A) XPS survey spectrum of synthesized N,S-CDs. (B–E) High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O1s, N1s and S2p. (F) Atomic percentage of elements in N,S-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, optimal excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs. Inset: images of N,S-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (B) Emission spectra of N,S-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm). (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of N,S-CDs. (D) pH effects on FL intensity of N,S-CDs.

(A) FL spectra of N,S-CDs adding various metal ions. (B) FL intensity change ratios (F0-F1)/F0 of N,S-CDs adding various metal ions. (C) Photos under 365 nm UV light (top) and sunlight (down) of N,S-CDs without (blank) and with 12 kinds of cations. (D) FL spectra of N,S-CDs adding different concentrations of Fe3+. Inset: fluorescence images of the N,S-CDs with various Fe3+ concentrations. (E) Plot of FL intensity change ratio versus Fe3+ concentrations (0–500 μM). (F) Linear plot of FL intensity change ratio versus Fe3+ concentrations (1–100 μM).

(A) Fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CDs with different concentration of Fe3+. (B) Zeta potential of N,S-CDs, Fe3+ and N,S-CDs + Fe3+. (C) Cyclic voltammetry of N,S-CDs. Inset: (ahν)2 vs hν curve of N,S-CDs. (D) Fe3+ recognition mechanism based on photo-induced electron transfer.

(A) Selectivity of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ for different substances (500 μM). (B) Influence of AA on FL intensity of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs/Fe3+. Inset: “on-off-on” fluorescence color changes of them under 365 nm UV light. (C) FL intensity of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ (300 μM) with AA concentration (0–300 μM) increasing. (D) The relationship between F/F1 and AA concentration from 0.5–90 μM (F and F1 are the FL intensities of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ with and without AA).
Schematic of one-step treatment to synthetize N,S-CDs for Fe3+ and AA detection.
“On-off-on” behavior mechanism of N,S-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ and AA.
Comparison of several typical fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Chitosan and κ-carrageenan-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles
Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: J. Xu and Y. Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study develops a high sensitive and selective “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of iron ion (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs), which were synthesized by using chitosan and κ-carrageenan as raw materials through one-step hydrothermal protocol. The synthesized N,S-CDs possess particularly high quantum yield (QY = 59.31%), excellent stability and excitation dependent behavior, showing great potential for practical applications. Furthermore, N,S-CDs provided high selectivity and strong anti-interference to Fe3+ due to its fluorescence quenching performance, revealing a wide linear concentration range from 1 to 100 μM for the detection of Fe3+ ion with an extremely low limit of detection of 57 nM, and presented reliable and accurate results in actual sample detection of Fe3+. The overall fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs with Fe3+ was due to the formation of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ initiated to the aggregation and electron transfer of N,S-CDs, resulting in the static quenching of fluorescence. More interestingly, AA could reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ and efficaciously recover the quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs/Fe3+. N,S-CDs/Fe3+ as “turn-on” fluorescent probe was further applied for detecting AA in a linear range of 0.5–90 μM with a detection limit of 38 nM.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have obtained broad attention in the past few years owing to their promising sensing applications [1–4]. Stable photoluminescence, surface modification flexibility, water solubility, low toxicity, and photo bleaching resistance are the most distinct advantages of CDs [5–7]. Many efforts have been devoted to explore the methods of synthesizing CDs, including laser ablation, arc discharge, chemical oxidation, microwave heating and hydrothermal synthesis methods, etc. [8–10]. Among them, the hydrothermal synthesis is a simple, green and effective method [11]. Although CDs can be simply obtained, how to adjust inherent characteristics, improve fluorescence properties and introduce more active sites for CDs is yet a tough task, which is certainly beneficial to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs in sensing application. Heteroatom doping is an optimal method to improve optical features and surface chemical properties of CDs [12,13]. Significantly, nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) doped CDs attract profound interest due to the atomic size, chemical valence, as well as the chemical inertness and low toxicity of nitrogen and sulfur atoms. For example, Xiao et al. developed a yellow-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots via hydrothermal heating of o-Phenylenediamine for the fast detection of glutathione [14]. Dong et al. synthesized highly fluorescent CDs with 2,3-diamino pyridine as the precursor and DMF as the solvent via a facile solvothermal process, and as a fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of nitrite ions and cellular imaging application [15]. Liu et al. synthesized N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots with L-cysteine and NH3·H2O, which was applied for pH and temperature sensing [16]. However, most CDs suffer from some drawbacks like low quantum yield (QY), moderate or even high toxic precursors, which hinder their broad applications. Thus, it is essential to search available precursors to synthetize heteroatom-doped CDs with high QY, low toxicity, even outstanding sensitivity and selectivity. Natural carbohydrate polymers are preferred to produce CDs not only because of their low cost, sustainability, biodegradability and biocompatibility, but also due to their functional groups which can modify CDs. Ghaemy et al. reported synthesis of CDs from a cheap and readily available carbohydrate polymers gum tragacanth [17]. Guo et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots with oxidized cellulose [18]. However, most reactions required adding small molecules containing heteroatoms, such as ethylenediamine, ammonia to obtain heteroatom-doped carbon dots. κ-Carrageenan (κ-Carr) is a natural polysaccharide extracted from red algae, which has been widely used in medical and food fields. κ-Carr contains a large amount of hydroxyl and sulfate, these functional groups are conducive to tune the structure of the CDs, resulting in the formation of functional CDs [19,20]. Chitosan is a deacetylated product of chitin, containing hydroxyl and amino, which are also favorable for the formation of CDs [21,22]. Therefore, κ-Carr and chitosan containing heteroatoms functional groups are potential sources to directly synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots.

The detection of Fe3+ is still of complete significance because iron ion is one of the most wide-ranging metal ions used in agricultural and industrial processes, which plays a significant part in biological systems, through binding with different regulatory proteins [23,24]. At present, various Fe3+ detection techniques including colorimetry, atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemical method and so on, require complex preparation processes and great expense [25,26]. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to develop a simple method for iron ion detection. Fluorescence assay is an economical, speedy and convenient testing approach, which has the unique advantages of excellent selectivity, brilliant sensitivity and real-time monitoring [27–29].

Herein, a low-cost method via selecting chitosan as carbon and nitrogen source, κ-Carr as carbon and sulfur source was constructed to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) through a simple hydrothermal route. The as-synthesized N,S-CDs emitted blue fluorescence. Based on the fluorescence quenching performance, N,S-CDs served as a prospective sensing probe for determination of Fe3+, and the selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference of Fe3+ detection were evaluated. More interestingly, when ascorbic acid was added into N,S-CDs/Fe3+, “off” state of fluorescent probe was recovered to “on” (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, the prepared N,S-CDs could be used as “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of iron ion (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA).
The detail information of materials and apparatus was provided in Supplementary Material.

N,S-CDs were synthetized through one-step hydrothermal route using chitosan and κ-carr. Foremost, 50 mg chitosan was dissolved in 10 mL 1% acetic acid solution and 50 mg of κ-carr was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 30 min. Then the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 18 h. Then the synthesized solution was cooled to room temperature, a brown solution with black sediment was obtained. The gained solution was centrifuging at speed of 10,000 rpm for 30 min and then filtered via 0.22 μm membrane to remove all precipitates. A light yellow N,S-CDs solution was acquired. Finally, the treated solution was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared N,S-CDs was utilized to detect a Fe3+ as a fluorescence sensor. The process of detecting Fe3+ was carried out as follows: the concentration of treated N,S-CDs dispersion was set to 100 μg/mL. Afterwards, various metal ion (Fe3+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Na+, Al3+, Cd2+ and Zn2+) was added respectively and the final concentration of each metal ion was 500 μM. Then, the solution was completely mixed and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with λex = 360 nm and slit width 2 nm. The sensitivity of detection Fe3+ was analyzed via recording the fluorescence intensity with different Fe3+ concentration (0–500 μM).

Adding Fe3+ (300 μM) into N,S-CDs (100 μg/mL) to build a fluorescence sensing platform: N,S-CDs/Fe3+. Then, the fluorescence responses of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ to various substances [Hg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Na+, Al3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, ascorbic acid (AA), melamine (Me), citric acid (CA), lysine (Lys), sucrose (Suc), glycine (Gly), glucose(Glu), glutamic acid (GA), ethanol (Et), oxalic acid (OA) and urea] were investigated to analyze the selectivity of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ sensing platform. And various concentration of AA (0–300 μM) was added to N,S-CDs/Fe3+ solution to analyze the sensitivity to AA.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was widely conducted to explore the morphological features and size of as-synthesized N,S-CDs. As observed from the TEM image in Fig. 1A, N,S-CDs were predominantly spherical, and the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1B) clearly exhibited a lattice spacing of 2.3 Å, which was ascribed to the (002) plane of carbon [30]. The average diameter was about 8 nm (Fig. 1C). Furthermore, XRD was utilized to explore crystallinity of N,S-CDs (Fig. 1D). The broad diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 21.06° represents to C (002) Bragg reflection (JCPDS No. 98-005-4232), which is similar to the graphite lattice spacing, belonging to amorphous carbon phase and partially graphitized structure [31]. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to evaluate the characteristic peaks of N,S-CDs in Fig. 1E. The strong bands at 3401 cm−1 and 3232 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of OH and NH [32]. The weak characteristic peak depicted at 1604 cm−1 was consistent with the stretching vibration of CC [33]. The absorption peak at 1404 cm−1 was ascribed to CN stretching vibration [34]. The sharp band at 1106 cm−1 was related with the C-N-C stretching vibration [35]. Above results confirmed the formation of N,S-CDs with a large amount of functional groups. Morphological feature and surface texture of N,S-CDs were further analyzed with Atomic Force Microscope (AFM), the typical 2D and 3D topographical view of N,S-CDs of sampling area 2.5 × 2.5 μm were represented in tapping Fig. 2A & B, respectively. The Fig. 2C demonstrated the average roughness of the N,S-CDs was 0.77 nm. Maximum profile peak height was 5.18 nm and valley depths was 1.04 nm. N,S-CDs was assessed by vertical depth analysis of the data and distribution of the product, which can draw the conclusion that N,S-CDs are spherical morphology.

The bonding environment and chemical states of N,S-CDs were ascertained by XPS analysis. The full scan XPS spectrum of N,S-CDs (Fig. 3A) revealed four characteristic peaks at 168.3, 284.7, 399.9 and 532.4 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [36]. In addition, high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s in Fig. 3B could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 285.3, 286 and 287.7 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-OH, C-S/C-N/C-O and CO, respectively [37]. The O1s signal (Fig. 3C) exhibited three components at 531.6, 532.4 and 533 eV, which are identified as CO, C-O-H and O-C=O [38]. The N1s XPS spectrum was shown in Fig. 3D, and the peaks at 399.8 and 401.1 eV are associated with CN and NH [36]. The S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3E) displayed triple peaks at 168.3, 168.8 and 169.5 eV, which are ascribed to C-S-O, -OSO3 2− (S 2p1/2) and -OSO3 2− (S 2p3/2) [39]. These spectral results indicated that N,S-CDs are made up by carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur with atomic percentages of 67.49%, 26.46%, 4.51% and 1.54%, respectively (Fig. 3F). Furthermore, XPS results further confirmed that nitrogen and sulfur atoms were doped in N,S-CDs and the surface of the prepared N,S-CDs was modified by some associated functional groups [40].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy were utilized to explore optical properties of N,S-CDs (Fig. 4A). The absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs displayed a slight characteristic peak around 280 nm, which could be ascribable to π-π* transition of CC bonds. The maximum excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths of N,S-CDs were 360 and 440 nm, respectively. The yellow solution of N,S-CDs displayed strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, which may be due to the band gap transitions of aromatic sp2 domains, quantum size effects and surface defects [41]. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs with various excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm) were illustrated in Fig. 4B, displaying a typical excitation-dependent behavior. The emission wavelength was red-shifted along with the increase of excitation wavelength. The emission intensity ascended as excitation wavelengths from 300 to 360 nm, and then descended from 360 to 390 nm. This behavior could be attributed to various surface states and different particle sizes [25]. Additionally, Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram exhibited emission color of N,S-CDs in Fig. 4C, indicating that with the increasing of excitation wavelengths, emission wavelengths were gradually red-shifted. CIE coordinate of max emission intensity was located at (0.17, 0.16), confirming the obvious blue emission of N, S-CD. The quantum yield (QY) of N,S-CDs was estimated to be 59.31%. The higher QY and fluorescence intensity may be due to the introduction of N and S atoms and functional groups of N, S-CD, which can induce new typical surface states [39].

The stability of N,S-CDs was evaluated by recording the influences of pH value, long-term storage, UV radiation and ionic strength on the emission intensity at 440 nm. No obvious changes were observed after storing for long time, irradiating with 365 nm UV light, or adding high ionic strength (0.5–2.0 M NaCl) as illustrated in Fig. S1A–C, and the fluorescence intensity remained nearly constant at the pH range of 3–10 (Fig. 4D). Such a high stability can completely fulfill the requests of actual sample detection.

Selectivity and sensitivity are two most essential indicators to reflect the sensing property of a probe. The emission spectra of N,S-CDs solution without (blank) and with 500 μM different metal ions including Fe3+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Na+, Al3+, Cd2+ and Zn2+ were depicted in Fig. 5A. Compared with other ions, Fe3+ ion induced a distinct fluorescence quenching to N,S-CDs. The fluorescence response change ratios of (F0-F1)/F0 were presented in Fig. 5B, where F0 and F1 are FL intensity of N,S-CDs at 440 nm without and with different metal ions. The higher (F0-F1)/F0 value indicated stronger quenched degree of N,S-CDs. It clearly proved that Fe3+ caused the strikingly stronger fluorescence quenching effect than other metal ions, indicating N,S-CDs as a fluorescence sensor has high selectivity to Fe3+. The selective quenching effect may be due to the stronger affinity between Fe3+ and N,S-CDs via faster chelation of Fe3+ to nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on N,S-CDs surfaces, further inducing the electron transfer between N,S-CDs and Fe3+ ion. Moreover, it could be observed clearly with naked eyes that only Fe3+ led to significant changes in FL intensity and color of N,S-CDs solution (Fig. 5C). In addition, Fe3+ changed the absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs because of its chelation with the surface functional groups of N,S-CDs, while other metal ions displayed negligible changes to the absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs as presented in Fig. S2. More importantly, the coexistence of interfering substance performed little influence on the response of N,S-CDs to Fe3+ (Fig. S3), revealing strong immunity to interference of N,S-CDs for monitoring Fe3+.

Simultaneously, the FL spectra of N,S-CDs with various Fe3+ concentrations (0–500 μM) were determined to survey the sensitivity of N,S-CDs for Fe3+ detection. It could be seen that the FL intensity maintained a downward trend along with the increase of Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 5D), and the plot of FL intensity change ratio versus Fe3+ concentration (0–500 μM) was presented in Fig. 5E. Moreover, the linear relationship was calculated via Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/
F 1
= 1 +
K SV
C
KSV and C represent the quenching constant and concentration of Fe3+, respectively. The linear correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9962 in the range of 1–100 μM (Fig. 5F). The limit of detection of N,S-CDs for Fe3+ ion was calculated to be 57 nmol/L, demonstrating that N,S-CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent sensor to detect Fe3+ ion.

Table 1 summarized the comparison between the as-synthesized N,S-CDs with some other CDs for Fe3+ ion detection, and revealed that N,S-CDs had comparable or even better quantum yield, sensing range and detection limit. Furthermore, the practical application of N,S-CDs as fluorescence sensor for determination of Fe3+ was performed by standard addition method. As displayed in Table S1, the recoveries were 109.87, 105.67, 104.46 and 101.10%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD %) was 0.56, 0.53, 1.09 and 0.90, which confirmed that this proposed method could provide reliable and accurate results in actual sample detection of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Various CDs possess distinctive functional groups, which lead to a variety of fluorescence quenching mechanisms, such as internal filtration effect (IFE), aggregation induced emission quenching or electron transfer [46]. The decay lifetime, Zeta potential and energy level simulation were utilized to investigate the possible quenching mechanism of Fe3+ toward N,S-CDs.

The decay curves of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs/Fe3+ were described in Fig. 6A, and the fluorescence decay lifetimes were estimated according to the double exponential fitting (Table S2). The τavg of N,S-CDs stayed constant at various concentration of Fe3+, which revealed the occurrence of a static fluorescence quenching mechanism. Additionally, the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ toward N,S-CDs exists the possibility of aggregation induced emission quenching, because the size of N,S-CDs was much increased when Fe3+ was added (Fig. S4). Fe3+ causes the Zeta potential of N,S-CDs change from −45.7 mV to −5.33 mV due to the strong electrostatic attraction between negatively charged N,S-CDs and positively charged Fe3+ (Fig. 6B), resulting in the decrease of electrostatic repulsion, as well as the aggregation of N,S-CDs.

Moreover, the non-radiative electron transfer fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by Fe3+ was proposed by energy level simulation. The lowest unoccupied molecular (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of N,S-CDs can be calculated by empirical formula:
E HOMO
= − e
E ox
+ 4.4
E LUMO
= − e
E red
+ 4.4
E red and E ox are onset of reduction and oxidation potentials of N,S-CDs. E red was measured to be −0.46 eV by using cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 6C), the relevant E LUMO was calculated to be −3.94 eV. Because oxidation behavior is irreversible, the optical energy band gap (E g) was utilized to determine the E HOMO:
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
−
E g

According to UV–vis absorption spectrum, E g was calculated as 4.32 eV (Fig. 6C inset), and EHOMO was −8.26 eV. As mentioned earlier, the crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE) of Fe3+ was 2.7 eV. After adding Fe3+, the complexation of N,S-CDs with Fe3+ led to the splitting of d-orbital of Fe3+. Electrons in the excited state of N,S-CDs can be partially transferred to the d-orbital of Fe3+. Consequently, electron transitions in the radiative form (fluorescence emission) of N,S-CDs were restrained, and non-radiative attenuation was dominant, leading to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 6D). Hence, the overall fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs with Fe3+ was depicted in Scheme 2 . When Fe3+ was added, the formation of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ initiated to the aggregation and electron transfer of N,S-CDs, resulting in the static quenching of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of N,S-CDs by AA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ with the addition of various substances were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 7A, the fluorescence recovery of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ by AA was the most obvious, while others were negligible, indicating that N,S-CDs/Fe3+ could be used as a selective probe to detect AA. In Fig. 7B, when AA was simply added into N,S-CDs alone, the fluorescence intensity had no difference with N,S-CDs (blank), showing that AA had no influence on N,S-CDs. However, after adding AA to N,S-CDs/Fe3+, the fluorescence turned on, which may be due to the special redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA [47]. Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+, inducing the fluorescence from “off” to “on” (Scheme 2). Moreover, the emission intensity of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ at around 440 nm enhanced gradually with the AA concentration from 0 to 300 μM (Fig. 7C), and a favorable linear relationship (R2 = 0.9881) was achieved in Fig. 7D in the range of 0.5–90 μM, with a detection limit of 38 nM. Therefore, a fluorescence probe with good selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of AA could be fabricated according to “on-off-on” system.

Briefly, novel N,S-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal treatment by using k-Carr and chitosan as raw materials for the first time. The as-synthesized N,S-CDs with average particle size of 8 nm displayed high quantum yield (QY = 59.31%), excellent stability and excitation-dependent behavior. The fluorescence of N,S-CDs was selectively quenched by Fe3+, which is attributed to the aggregation and electron transfer of N,S-CDs induced by the formation of N,S-CDs/Fe3+, and then the quenched fluorescence was effectively restored by AA due to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. Therefore, N,S-CDs as an “on-off-on” fluorescence sensor was fabricated for sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA. The linear detection range for Fe3+ and AA was determined as 1–100 μM, and 0.5–90 μM, as well as relatively low detection limit of 57 nmol/L and 38 nmol/L, respectively. Moreover, this method constructed here not only showed excellent specificity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability but also performed the quantitative detection of Fe3+ and AA with satisfactory results, demonstrating its potential for practical application in detection.

Data curation, Writing, original draft preparation: Jiyao Xu and Yesheng Wang; Formal analysis: Yesheng Wang, Lili Sun and Quan Qi; Investigation: Xihui Zhao; Methodology: Xihui Zhao; Project administration: Xihui Zhao; Resources: Xihui Zhao; Supervision: Xihui Zhao; Writing- Reviewing and Editing: Xihui Zhao.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41, No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2021.09.165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, K.R. Aseer, S. Perumal, N. Karthik, Y.R. Lee, Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink, Biosens. Bioelectron. 99 (2018) 303–311. doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.07.076.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile ultrasonic synthesized NH2-carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive Co2+ ion detection and cell imaging
